design_philosophy.core_loop: Two-phase infiltration: survive scrolling shooter sequence to reach fortress air lock, then navigate maze interior to locate and destroy core while escaping before detonation
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Dual-mode difficulty spike: arcade precision in approach phase vs. navigation/combat challenge in fortress exploration; time pressure during escape sequence creates climactic tension
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Solo warrior infiltrating massive enemy war machines, combining piloting skill with strategic exploration and tactical combat
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Uncompromising hybrid design that refuses to pick between genres; shooter and adventure phases have fundamentally different mechanics and challenges. Fortress layout randomization (exit never in same location) forces genuine navigation puzzles rather than memorization. Game expects player mastery of both arcade reflexes and adventure-game spatial reasoning.

difficulty.lives_system: Three attempts per air base approach sequence; destruction of lightship forces restart from beginning (Japanese version) or from destruction point with life penalty (American version)
difficulty.checkpointing: Regional variation: Japanese version resets full approach sequence on any collision; American version allows mid-sequence respawn. No checkpoints within fortress exploration segments.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Password system allows players to skip completed fortresses and attempt harder ones directly; implies intended replayability at specific difficulty tiers rather than linear forced progression

progression.upgrade_system: Energy pickups and limited 'crash beam bullets' (more powerful ammunition) collectible during approach phase; ammunition must be conserved for fortress interior combat
progression.unlock_mechanism: Password system tied to fortress completion; designer reference password 'SUGA' unlocks final fortress, suggesting passwords are progression keys rather than save states
replayability_hooks: Fortress interiors appear procedurally laid out with variable exit locations, preventing route memorization
Password system allows challenge runs at specific difficulty fortresses
Ammunition management creates strategic variation in loadout between runs
Dual-phase design means each fortress completion requires two distinct skill demonstrations

world_building_techniques: Opening story establishes threat: locust-like air fortresses consuming all resources
Single-warrior protagonist framing creates high-stakes narrative justification for solo infiltration
Environment progression (multiple sequentially harder fortresses) implies escalating threat
Designer name embedding (Hiroaki Suga's 'SUGA' password) creates metatextual connection between narrative and development

boss_design.philosophy: Central fortress core serves as mandatory objective rather than traditional boss encounter; destruction is mechanical task requiring navigation to specific location rather than combat gauntlet
notable_bosses: Fortress Core (objective-based destruction rather than combat-phase boss)
Unnamed enemy robots and security devices within fortress interiors

faction_indicators: Space Federation (humanity's unified defensive force, defeated by fortresses, sends solo warrior)
Hal Bailman (protagonist identity establishes HAL Laboratory connection through name homage)
Air Fortresses (alien/hostile faction with locust-swarm behavior pattern)
danger_signals: Fortress explosion sequence creates time-pressure danger state post-core destruction
Three-attempt limit on approach phase signals permadeath consequence design
Interior maze navigation without map implies disorientation as intended threat
environmental_storytelling: Air fortress interior architecture implies massive scale and systematic design
Enemy robot and security device placement suggests organized defensive systems
Required escape sequence after destruction establishes fortress as active threat even when disabled

sources_used: tcrf - Air Fortress (The Cutting Room Floor)
